contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- What an [[exception]] (Exception) is
- How to catch and handle errors using try-catch statements
- How to write code that always executes using the [[finally]] clause

Exception handling is the "safety mechanism" of a program. By preparing for errors, you create robust programs.

contents.title: Exception Class Hierarchy
contents.body:
Java's exception [[class]]es are organized in the following hierarchy.

Throwable (parent of all exceptions)
├── Error (serious errors; do not catch)
│   ├── OutOfMemoryError
│   └── [[StackOverflowError]]
└── Exception (exceptions programs should handle)
    ├── **Checked exceptions**
    │   ├── IOException
    │   ├── SQLException
    │   └── FileNotFoundException
    └── **RuntimeException** (**Unchecked exceptions**)
        ├── **NullPointerException**
        ├── ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
        ├── NumberFormatException
        ├── IllegalArgumentException
        └── [[ClassCastException]]

| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| Checked exception | The compiler enforces handling (try-catch or throws required) |
| Unchecked exception | Handling is not enforced (often caused by program bugs) |
| Error | JVM-level issues; normally not caught |
contents.note: "**Checked exceptions**" means "exceptions the compiler checks (enforces)." Direct subclasses of Exception are [[checked exceptions]], while those under [[RuntimeException]] are **unchecked exceptions**.

contents.title: The try-catch-finally Statement
contents.body: Wrap code that may throw an exception in a try block and handle it with catch.
contents.note: You can write multiple catch blocks, but subclass exceptions must come first. Writing Exception before NumberFormatException will cause a [[compile]] error.

contents.title: Difference Between Checked and Unchecked Exceptions
contents.body:
Java exceptions are broadly divided into two types.

| Type | Parent Class | Catch Required | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| Checked exception | Exception | Required | IOException, SQLException |
| Unchecked exception | RuntimeException | Optional | NullPointerException, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException |

Checked exceptions are for "predictable errors," while unchecked exceptions are for "program bugs."
contents.note: Errors (such as OutOfMemoryError) are normally not caught. They are unrecoverable system errors.

contents.title: throw and throws
contents.body: Use throw to throw an exception and throws to declare that a [[method]] may throw an exception.
contents.note: **RuntimeException** (**unchecked exceptions**) do not require a throws declaration. Checked exceptions must either be handled with try-catch or propagated with throws to the caller.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- Throwable → Error / Exception → **RuntimeException** hierarchy
| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| Checked exception | Compiler enforces handling (IOException, etc.) |
| Unchecked exception | Handling is optional (NullPointerException, etc.) |
- Multiple catch blocks are allowed (write subclasses first)
- throw: throws an exception, [[throws]]: declares the method's exception

quizzes.question: Arrange the exception handling blocks in the order they execute (when an exception occurs).
quizzes.explanation: When an exception occurs, the execution order is: (1) the try block executes up to the point of the exception → (2) the matching catch block handles the exception → (3) the finally block always executes → (4) code after the try-catch-finally runs.

quizzes.question: Which of the following is a checked exception?
quizzes.explanation: IOException is a checked exception. It is a direct subclass of Exception, not a subclass of RuntimeException. NullPointerException and NumberFormatException are RuntimeExceptions (unchecked), and OutOfMemoryError is an Error.

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: "A" is output in the try block, then 10/0 throws an ArithmeticException and "B" is skipped. "C" is output in the catch block. "D" is output in the finally block. "E" is output after the try-catch. The result is "ACDE".

lessons.title: try-with-resources and Multi-catch
lessons.summary: Automatic resource management and modern exception handling

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- [[try-with-resources]]: Syntax for automatically closing resources
- Multi-catch: Handling multiple [[exception]]s in a single catch block
- Convenient exception handling features since Java 7

These are essential techniques for preventing resource management mistakes in practice.

contents.title: The try-with-resources Statement
contents.body: Resources that implement the [[AutoCloseable]] [[interface]] are automatically closed.
contents.note: [[Class]]es used with try-with-resources must implement the AutoCloseable or Closeable interface. This includes [[InputStream]], [[OutputStream]], Reader, Writer, [[Connection]], etc.

contents.title: What Happens Without try-with-resources
contents.body:
The traditional try-[[finally]] approach had the following issues:

- Forgetting to call close() causes resource leaks
- close() itself can throw an exception
- Code becomes verbose and hard to read

With try-with-resources, resources that implement AutoCloseable are automatically closed.
contents.note: Since Java 9, [[variable]]s declared outside the try can also be used with try-with-resources (if they are effectively final).

contents.title: Multi-catch (Java 7+)
contents.body: Multiple exceptions can be handled in a single catch block.
contents.note: The variable e in a multi-catch is implicitly final. You cannot reassign e within the catch block.

contents.title: Relationship Between finally and return
contents.body:
finally always executes regardless of exceptions, but be careful about its interaction with return.

- Even if try or catch has a return, finally still executes
- A return in finally overrides the try/catch return (not recommended)
- If an exception occurs in finally, exceptions from try/catch are lost

Typical exam question:
  try { return 1; }
  finally { return 2; }
  → Return value is 2 (finally overrides)

Best practices:
- Do not write return in finally
- Use finally only for cleanup operations like closing resources
- Use try-with-resources for resource management
contents.note: A return in finally silently swallows any exception thrown in try or catch. Some IDEs issue a compiler warning for return in finally.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- try-with-resources: Automatically closes AutoCloseable resources
- Resources are closed in reverse order of declaration
- Multi-catch: catch (A | B e) combines multiple exceptions
- Parent-child exceptions cannot be multi-caught
- The variable e in multi-catch is implicitly final
- A return in finally overrides try/catch returns (not recommended)
- try-with-resources is a complete replacement for manual finally close

quizzes.question: Which interface must a class implement to be automatically closed in try-with-resources?
quizzes.explanation: In try-with-resources, instances of classes that implement the AutoCloseable interface are automatically closed. Closeable is a sub-interface of AutoCloseable that only throws IOException. Classes implementing either can be used with try-with-resources.

quizzes.question: What is the return value of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: return 1 is executed in the try block, but since finally always executes, the return 2 in finally overrides try's return 1. As a result, 2 is returned. Using return in finally is not recommended.

quizzes.question: Which line causes a compile error in the following code?
quizzes.explanation: A compile error occurs on line 4. Parent-child exceptions cannot be combined in a multi-catch. Since NumberFormatException is a subclass of Exception, catch (Exception | NumberFormatException e) is invalid. You need to use either Exception alone or NumberFormatException alone.

quizzes.question: Complete the exception handling for a file reading process. Catch IOException and display a completion message in finally.
quizzes.explanation: This is the basic try-catch-finally syntax. Write code that may throw exceptions in the try block, catch specific exception types with catch. The finally block always executes regardless of whether an exception occurred.

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- How to create custom [[exception]] [[class]]es
- Choosing between **checked exceptions** and **unchecked exceptions**
- How to propagate the cause using [[exception chaining]] (cause)

You'll be able to define exceptions tailored to your business logic.

contents.title: Custom Checked Exception
contents.body: Create a **checked exception** by [[inheriting]] from Exception.
contents.note: Adding detailed error information as fields to [[custom exceptions]] allows the catch side to retrieve useful information.

contents.title: Naming Conventions for Custom Exceptions
contents.body:
- Exception class names must always end with "~Exception"
- For **checked exceptions**: Extend Exception
- For **unchecked exceptions**: Extend [[RuntimeException]]
- Provide a [[constructor]] that accepts a message argument

Examples: InsufficientBalanceException, UserNotFoundException, etc.

contents.title: Custom Unchecked Exception
contents.body: Create an **unchecked exception** by extending RuntimeException.
contents.note: In modern Java, there's a trend toward preferring **unchecked exceptions** (**RuntimeException**). The Spring Framework also extensively uses custom RuntimeExceptions.

contents.title: Exception Chaining
contents.body: A mechanism to wrap the original exception as the cause of another exception.
contents.note: Exception chaining is used for "exception translation between layers." Convert a DAO's SQLException to a Service's DataAccessException so each layer can deal with exceptions at the appropriate level of abstraction.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- Custom **checked exception**: extends Exception (handling enforced)
- Custom **unchecked exception**: extends **RuntimeException** (handling optional)
- Pass the error message to the parent class with super(message)
- Custom fields can hold detailed error information
- Exception chaining: new MyException(msg, cause) preserves the cause exception
- getCause() retrieves the original exception
- Use exception chaining for exception translation between layers

quizzes.question:
To create a checked exception, what class should you extend?
public class MyException extends _____ {
quizzes.explanation: To create a checked exception, extend the Exception class. Extending RuntimeException creates an unchecked exception. Checked exceptions require the caller to use try-catch or declare throws.

quizzes.question: Which method retrieves the cause exception in exception chaining?
quizzes.explanation: The getCause() method retrieves the cause (original exception). In exception chaining, the cause exception is passed via the constructor as new MyException("msg", cause), and retrieved with getCause(). This is defined in the Throwable class so it's available for all exceptions.

quizzes.question: In which case is an unchecked exception appropriate?
quizzes.explanation: Unchecked exceptions (RuntimeException) are used to indicate program bugs or invalid states. Passing null or an out-of-range ID is a programming error. File not found or network disconnection are external factors, so checked exceptions are appropriate.

choices.text: When a file is not found
When a network connection is lost
When null is passed to a method

lessons.title: Exception Handling Best Practices
lessons.summary: What to do and what to avoid

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Anti-patterns to avoid (empty catch blocks, catch-all Exception)
- Proper [[exception]] handling design principles
- Combining logging and exception handling

This knowledge is essential for leveling up from "code that works" to "good code."

contents.title: Anti-Patterns (What Not to Do)
contents.body: The following code patterns should be avoided in practice.
contents.note: "Swallowing exceptions" is the most serious anti-pattern. It makes debugging in production environments extremely difficult.

contents.title: Exception Handling Patterns to Avoid
contents.body:
Patterns to absolutely avoid in practice:

- Empty catch blocks: Silently swallow errors
- catch(Exception e): Catches all exceptions indiscriminately
- Replacing exceptions with return null: Causes [[NullPointerException]] at the caller
- Using exceptions for control flow: Don't use exceptions for normal branching

Exceptions are for "exceptional" processing. Use [[if statements]] for normal branching.

contents.title: Best Practices (Proper Exception Handling)
contents.body: Recommended exception handling patterns for practice.
contents.note: Objects.requireNonNull() was introduced in Java 7. If the argument is null, it immediately throws a NullPointerException, helping detect bugs early.

contents.title: Exception Handling Checklist
contents.body:
Rules to follow in practice:

| Rule | Details |
|---|---|
| Don't swallow exceptions | At minimum, output to logs |
| Catch specific exceptions | Don't broadly catch Exception |
| Use try-with-resources | Avoid manual finally close |
| Don't use exceptions for control flow | Don't use exceptions for things achievable with if/for |
| Leverage exception chaining | Preserve the cause exception when re-throwing |
| No return in finally | It overrides try/catch results |
| Choose the appropriate exception type | Consider custom exceptions |
| Make error messages specific | Not "Error" but "User ID: 123 not found" |
| Prevent NullPointerException | Use Optional and requireNonNull |

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- Never swallow exceptions (empty catch blocks)
- Catch specific exception types instead of broad Exception
- Use [[try-with-resources]] for resource management
- Preserve the cause exception with [[exception chaining]] when re-throwing
- Don't use exceptions for control flow
- Use Objects.requireNonNull() for null checks
- Never use return in [[finally]]

quizzes.question: Which is the most serious exception handling anti-pattern?
quizzes.explanation: Swallowing exceptions (empty catch blocks) is the most serious anti-pattern. Since errors are neither recorded nor handled, identifying bugs in production environments becomes extremely difficult. At the very minimum, log output should be performed.

choices.text: Building exception messages with string concatenation

quizzes.question: What are the anti-patterns in the following code?
quizzes.explanation: There are two anti-patterns: (1) Catching the overly broad Exception type. IOException and NumberFormatException should be caught separately. (2) The error message is just "Error" with no specific information. You can't tell what happened or where.

choices.text: Exception is too broad & message is vague

quizzes.question: What is the recommended way to verify that a method argument is not null?
quizzes.explanation: Objects.requireNonNull(arg, message) was added in Java 7. If the argument is null, it immediately throws a NullPointerException. Used at the beginning of methods for argument validation, it helps detect bugs early.

lessons.title: Practical Exception Handling Patterns
lessons.summary: Exception handling patterns commonly used in practice

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Using [[exception]]s for input validation
- Retry processing (retrying on transient errors)
- Combining [[transaction]]s with exception handling

Let's master patterns used in real-world development.

contents.title: Input Validation and Exceptions
contents.body: A pattern that leverages exceptions for user input validation.
contents.note: IllegalArgumentException is a standard **unchecked exception** used when invalid arguments are passed to a [[method]].

contents.title: Practical Principles of Exception Handling
contents.body:
Practical principles for exception handling:

- Catch exception types as specific as possible
- Include information in exception messages that identifies the cause
- Log the exception before handling it
- For recoverable cases, respond with retries or default values
- For unrecoverable cases, propagate appropriately to the caller

contents.title: Retry Pattern
contents.body: Exception handling with retry logic for transient failures such as network communication.
contents.note: Gradually increasing the retry interval (exponential backoff) is the best practice in real-world development. Retrying immediately can overload the server.

contents.title: Exception Handling Summary
contents.body:
Complete overview of exception handling:

【Syntax】
| Keyword | Function |
|---|---|
| try-catch-finally | Basic exception handling |
| try-with-resources | Automatic resource close |
| Multi-catch | catch (A \| B e) |
| throw | Throw an exception |
| throws | Declare method exceptions |

【Exception Types】
| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| Checked exception | IOException, SQLException |
| Unchecked exception | NullPointerException, IllegalArgumentException |
| Error | OutOfMemoryError (don't catch) |

【Patterns】
| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| Custom exception | Express business-specific errors |
| Exception chaining | Preserve cause exception on re-throw |
| Retry | Handling transient failures |
| Validation | Prevent exceptions with pre-checks |

【Rules】
- Don't swallow exceptions, don't catch broadly
- Prefer [[try-with-resources]], no return in [[finally]]

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
| Pattern | Description |
|---|---|
| Input validation | Immediately reject invalid arguments with IllegalArgumentException |
| Retry pattern | Wait and retry for transient failures |
| Exponential backoff | Gradually increase the retry interval |
| Thread.currentThread().interrupt() | Re-set after InterruptedException |
- Exceptions are for "exceptional cases" only; don't use them for normal control flow

quizzes.question: Which exception class should be used when invalid arguments are passed to a method?
quizzes.explanation: IllegalArgumentException is a standard RuntimeException used when invalid or inappropriate arguments are passed to a method. For example, when -1 is passed to an argument that doesn't allow negative values. NullPointerException is specific to null, and IllegalStateException is for when an object's state is invalid.

quizzes.question: What is the technique of gradually increasing retry intervals called?
quizzes.explanation: Exponential Backoff is a technique that exponentially increases the retry interval, such as 1s → 2s → 4s → 8s. This prevents excessive load on the server during failures. It is a standard practice adopted in cloud service SDKs.

quizzes.question: Select all correct exception handling best practices.
quizzes.explanation: "Catch specific exception classes," "manage resources with try-with-resources," and "preserve the cause exception with exception chaining" are best practices. "Always use printStackTrace() when catching an exception" is insufficient; a proper logging framework (Logger) should be used instead.

choices.text: Always use printStackTrace() when catching an exception
Preserve the cause exception with exception chaining
